content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 23, 1990

Dorothy S. Maron
Dorothy S. Maron Re-Election Committee
P.O. Box 5306
Oxnard, CA 93031

RE: Your Request for
Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-202

Dear Ms. Maron:

You have requested advice concerning the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Your questions are hypothetical or general in nature, therefore we consider
your request to be for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c) (copy enclosed).²

QUESTIONS

1. You have a committee established for your city council
seat. If you decide to run for mayor, are you required to
establish a separate committee?

2. May you transfer funds from your city council committee
to your committee for mayor?

3. How do you handle contributions received by you when the
contributor does not specify which committee they are intended
for?

_______________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

A-90-202
Page 2


        4.    If you are the subject of a recall as city councilwoman,
may you run for mayor at the same time?

        5.    As the subject of a recall, can you use the funds you
have in your current committee? Can you raise and spend defense
funds under your current city council committee ID number?


                              CONCLUSIONS

        1.    You must establish a separate committee and bank account
to run for mayor.

        2.    You may transfer funds between your own controlled com-
mittees.

        3.    When contributions received are not designated by the
contributor for a particular controlled committee, you may allo-
cate the contribution to any of your controlled committees.

        4.    The Act does not address the issue of the ability of an
officeholder who is the subject of a recall effort to seek elec-
tion to another office. You should contact the Secretary of
State, Elections Division or a private attorney regarding this
matter.

        5.    You may use contributions received in connection with a
previous election to the same office to oppose the recall. The
contributions received into this account would be subject to the
contribution limits imposed by Proposition 73.


                              ANALYSIS

        The Act, as amended by Proposition 73, requires that a
candidate must establish one campaign bank account for each of-
fice. (Section 85201.) Section 84101 requires candidates to file
a statement of organization within 10 days after receiving
contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year. Commission regulations further require the establishment of a separate
controlled committee for each campaign bank account:

        When a candidate is required to file a statement of
organization for a controlled committee pursuant to Govern-
ment Code Section 84101, the candidate shall establish a
separate controlled committee for each specific office
identified in statements filed by the candidate pursuant to
Government Code Section 85200.

        Regulation 18521 (copy enclosed).

A-90-202
Page 3


Regarding transfers between a candidate's controlled commit-
tees, on May 15, 1989, the United States District Court, Eastern
District of California enjoined the Commission from enforcing that
portion of Section 85304 which prohibited a candidate or of-
ficeholder from transferring funds among his or her own controlled
committees. (SEIU, AFL-CIO, CLC, et al. v. FEC, Case No. 89-
0433.) Therefore, you may transfer funds from your city council
committee to a committee you establish to run for mayor.3

Section 85201 also requires that all expenditures in connection with a particular election be made from the bank account
designated for that election. Funds held in a campaign bank account designated for your city council election should be
transferred to the bank account designated for the mayoral election before being used for expenses associated with the mayoral
election. This section also provides that all contributions or
loans made to the candidate, to a person on behalf of the
candidate, or to the candidate's controlled committee shall be
deposited in the account established for that office. Commission
regulations further state that:

When a contributor makes a contribution or loan to a
candidate with more than one controlled committee and does
not designate the committee for which the contribution or
loan is made, the candidate or his or her designee may allocate the contribution or loan to any one of the candidate's
controlled committees....

Regulation 18523(a)
(copy enclosed).

However, when soliciting funds, please note that Regulation
18523.1 (copy enclosed) requires:

When a candidate or controlled committee of a candidate
makes any written solicitation for contributions, the
solicitation shall identify the particular controlled committee by name, the specific office and the particular election
for which contributions are sought, and shall instruct
contributors to designate their contributions for that
particular controlled committee.




3
The contribution limits apply in the aggregate to all committees controlled by the same candidate other than committees formed
in connection with a special election to fill a vacant elective
office. (Slussky Advice Letter, No. A-90-024, copy enclosed.)

A-90-202
Page 4


     Addressing the issue of which funds may be used for recall
defense, expenses incurred by an officeholder who is the subject
of a recall fall into two categories. First, they can be defined
as "expenses associated with holding office" as referred to in
Section 85202(b). In addition, they can be defined as expenses in
connection with a ballot measure. Therefore, the funds from your
city council committee may be used to oppose the recall. Ad-
ditional funds may be contributed to that account for the purpose
of the recall. All funds received in this account would be
subject to the contribution limits. In addition, once you have
been served with a notice of intention to circulate a recall peti-
tion and the notice has been published or posted in accordance
with Elections Code Section 27007 or other applicable law, a
separate committee may be established to raise contributions to
oppose the recall. Those contributions are not subject to the
contribution limitations. However, the funds must be deposited in
an account separate from any other campaign account(s) and may not
be used for other election campaign or officeholder expenses.
(Hong Advice Letter, No. A-89-133; Roberti Advice Letter, No. A-
89-358, copies enclosed.)

     If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Kathryn E. Donovan
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Sandra L. Taylor
                                            Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures
